topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The music was too loud, a grating soundtrack to the forced merriment. I watched from the edge of the patio, nursing a lukewarm glass of white wine, my jaw tight enough to crack. Then I saw him. Standing near the buffet, laughing with a woman whose dress shimmered under the chandeliers, was Mark. My stomach clenched, a sudden, sharp tightening that made my hands tremble slightly around the glass. He turned, his eyes sweeping the room, and for a fraction of a second, they locked onto mine. The smile he offered was practiced, wide, and utterly devoid of the warmth I remembered. I took a slow, deliberate sip, the liquid burning a path down my throat. I didn't move, didn't breathe deeply. I just watched him, cataloging every slight shift in his posture, every calculated glance, feeling a deep, internal pressure building behind my ribs, a hot, restless energy that demanded release.

[story 2]

"Oh, look who decided to grace us with their presence," I muttered under my breath, the words tasting like grit. I was leaning against a marble pillar, trying to appear engrossed in the floral arrangements, but my gaze kept snagging on him across the crowded ballroom. He was unmistakable, dressed impeccably, radiating a kind of effortless success that always grated on my nerves. When our eyes met, the easy conviviality he projected faltered, replaced by a flicker of something guarded, something that looked suspiciously like recognition mixed with immediate distance. I felt a sudden, unwelcome heat rise up my neck. I straightened up abruptly, the movement sharp, and turned my back to the scene, focusing instead on the intricate pattern of the wallpaper, willing the sensation of his presence to simply evaporate.

[story 3]

The air in the reception hall felt thick, suffocating. Every forced cheer and clinking glass seemed amplified, grating against my nerves like sandpaper. I spotted him near the dance floor, a familiar silhouette against the flashing lights. A sudden, intense wave washed over me, making my vision narrow just slightly. I gripped my clutch bag so tightly my knuckles turned white. When he finally turned toward me, there was a momentary stillness in his expression—a pause before the performance resumed. I met his gaze head-on, refusing to avert my eyes. My voice, when I finally spoke, was low, clipped, and carried a brittle edge that I immediately regretted. "Fancy seeing you here." The sound of my own voice felt alien, sharp, and entirely too loud in that charged silence.
